title: Teramnus repens
intro: Teramnus repens, also known as the sensitive plant or touch-me-not, is a perennial legume native to tropical regions. The plant is known for its health benefits, which include anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, making it useful in treating various ailments such as skin conditions and wounds. The bioactive constituents of Teramnus repens include flavonoids, alkaloids, and glycosides, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, the plant is commonly used to prepare teas, infusions, and extracts, with the most common herbal preparations being the dried leaves and roots used as a decoction or tincture to treat various health issues.
taxonomy: Teramnus repens is classified within the plant kingdom, specifically within the family Fabaceae, commonly known as the legume family. This family is a large and diverse group of plants, including beans, peas, and many other types of legumes. Within the Fabaceae family, Teramnus repens is further classified within the subfamily Faboideae. This subfamily is characterized by its complex flowers and seed pods, which are typical features of many legume species. The exact classification of Teramnus repens is as follows: genus - Teramnus, species - T. repens.
uses: Teramnus repens is known to have various medicinal uses, particularly in traditional medicine. The plant is said to have anti-inflammatory properties, which can be used to treat various ailments such as fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. The roots of the plant are also believed to have antiseptic properties, making them useful for treating wounds and infections. In some cultures, the plant is used to treat respiratory issues such as coughs and colds. However, further research is needed to confirm the effectiveness and safety of using Teramnus repens for medicinal purposes.

side_effects: Teramnus repens is reported to be mildly toxic, and its seeds contain a compound called rotenone which can cause side effects such as nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea if ingested in large quantities. Prolonged exposure to the plant's toxic compounds may also lead to liver and kidney damage. However, the exact severity and prevalence of these side effects are still being studied. Individuals who handle the plant extensively, such as farmers and laborers, may be at a higher risk of exposure.
